author: @ceejayc6502
text: So you are actually not willing to even discuss the instant blindness some can get in a few nano seconds even from a laser reflection? Despite 4-5 well meaning comments? Not even for the safety of your viewers? That's an unsub. Shame on you.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
I am currently investigating using a laser fitted to a CNC to produce PCBs. One of my concerns is reflections from the target damaging the laser diode. I am considering fitting a fan with air filter to suck away fumes generated by the cutting, before they are deposited on the laser optics. 
Laser protection glasses are ok for a single user, but something else must be provided if there are going to be more people in the room when the laser is active. This assumes that the protection glasses provided are acquit for the task. It is said that at least some of the laser protection glasses sold on web sites like Amazon and eBay may not provide the level of protection advertised. 
The are YouTube channels that have demonstrated such short comings using a laser power meter to test a range of protection glasses. There can be nothing worse than thinking you are using the correct protection, when in fact it is useless. 
 At 30 watts, even a momentary reflection into the eye will result in blindness. 
I will be very interested in your experiences with the laser. I was going to ask about the laser specification, such as focal length, focused beam width and wavelength. But I can look that up from the product reference.  

As a professional user, with responsibility for worker safety, there will be regulations to follow regarding laser safety. Have you investigated the regulations associated with using such a powerful laser in a works environment?

author: @davidprice875
text: I have a similar diode laser based unit, the Ortur Laser Master 2. The power consumption is 20W but the laser emiitter is 5 - 5.5W at 450nm. After getting a good focus it will cut paper, card and 3mm black acrylic (going round 5 times). That frequency is not absorbed by clear acrylic so won't cut.  Plywood is very problematic, the problem is the glue, the supposedly laser ply I tried cut through the first wood layer but not through the glue so it just chars.  I plan on trying some laser grade mdf and also a G8 lens to replace the stock G2 lens to see if that improves things.   Don't rely on those visors, best to fully enclose the thing while in operation.

author: @davidprice875
text: Robert, for your K40 have a look at K40 Whisperer, it can be installed on Windows, Mac and Linux and doesn't need a dongle.

author: @IslandHermit
text: Back when you were making CR2023 cells using structured carbon from hemp you used a circular die to cut out the different layers for the cell. It occurred to me at the time that while that worked great for a single layer of active material to stack multiple layers you really want to leave a tab on one side of the anode and cathode layers so that they can be tied together. Something like this laser cutter would be perfect for that.

author: @williamarmstrong7199
text: The death adaptor plug well known to be highly dangerous BIG Clive has done a lot of reviews of their compleat lack of safety.

author: @michaelbiggs7987
text: Looks like those diode laser cutters use input power in their specs, rather than optical output power, so the "20W laser" actually puts out 5.5W of laser power. Now 5.5W is still a lot of power, but it's not going to compare to a 20W CO2 laser, which is a real 20W. But at that price, if you can get it to cut thin wood or plastic it might still be useful.

author: @hootsmin
text: All these karens worried about eyes burning from the scary lazer, I suppose you have not thought to wear eye protection to save your eyes from the blue-white phosphor coated 450nm LED's that are on all the SMART streetlamps and the cars and perhaps in your home?  Those are at 666THz the correct frequency to destroy the macular cells in your eyes at maximum speed.  In the land of the blind, the one-eyed man is king.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: ah interesting getting to the point that shows it as an arm hanging out with the laser moving back and forth on that open ended arm, mine is trapped between two sides that have motors on both ends that I guess mirror power to allow them to move the same direction on their independent belts :) (also I bought more Vee slot and built a bigger unit now and it seems to be working fine even at the 12 inch by 17 inch range of motion I have now :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: something else about mine is that the beam is not straight when it is finalized but rather a cone, to bring it down to a fine point of power, and I would like to use a reflector that is concave or convex *depending on if just after or just before hitting that pinch point but so close as to get as much power packed into as tiny a dot* so I can readjust the beam's  photons paths so they are in a straight line, that way I will not be limited by where that pinch point is, and can let it fly deep into a kerf while jetting air down in there and clearing out anything that does not fully ablate away as a gas or smoke :) Not sure if yours will have this issue of a beam conversion point, or not.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I don't know mate - I have done a little cutting of paper but have a couple of jobs I want it to do so I will be trying those as and when in the next few days

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
still watching but if this is a 455nm I am definitely jelly, LOL I only have a 15 watt one XD, (though thanks to buying a second head because I thought I was going to get screwed out of the head for the first one I tried to by *serious story here if anyone wants to know about one particular pitfall that sellers can use to weasel out of refunds, if anyone wants to know* anyway, I ended up solving the issue thanks to things like google maps and such, :) so now I have two heads as I got one finally when I bought the second unit with the refund money I got back, (they shipped me literally everything except the head, cross bar of VeeSlot and hardware to hold the head on, and the little PCB and USB to serial cable (there is a USB to standard serial converter chip in that little USB plug) so basically my hope is that one day I can get a combiner cube (two prisms that make a cube when put together on their 45 degree angle that has a coating on one side, that causes the beam to reflect off of the 45 degree angle while the other side the beam passes through both angle and flats thus you can put both beams right on top of each other instead of beside each other like knife edging, though there are losses because of the material you are shooting it through in both instances that I guess polarize the beam in some curious way to allow this, (DLP projectors do this too in some instances where the beams of three colors bounce off of 3 different DLP chips for each color, and then through a cube that is basically 4 prisms so that each can be bounced from sides of 2 colors while the last one passes right through from back to front of the cube, so when overlaid you get the appearance of "white" light if the power matches, which is really cool, but those are color dependent on reflecting, while the combiner I am after is not, :)  

anyway my hope is I will get a 30 watt input to the cube (two 15 watt pulses) and the output side of the cube should be like 25 to 27 or so watts *due to the portion of wattage absorbed by the cube and re-emitted as something like thermal IR or something :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there is no earth mate - one isn't needed - on the power brick side it is a class 2 electrically isolated piece of equipment on the dc side it is such allow voltage as to be harmless - if you fit an earth you are in fact making it more dangerous - so really don't do that!

Rob Nice To See You In The Laser Diode Gang.. I Have A 5w A3 Cutter.. The Glasses That You Get With The Kit.. You May Wish To Replace As They Don't Have A OD Rating.. All Laser Glasses Should Be Marked With This At Least. 
What This Means You Think That The Laser Glasses Are Protecting Your Eyesight And They Are Not..!
Better To Be Safe Than Sorry.. Links Below Rob Unless YouTube Has Put Them In The Spam..
Regards Nige

author: @michaelclayton632
text: In 1977, my senior year in high school, my physics teacher borrowed a laser from a friend of his at the University of British Columbia for a class  demonstration. When he gave the demonstration,  he explained that laser scientists bragged amongst each other about the power of their lasers in Gillettes.  This came about from them stating how many Gillette Blue blades they could blast a hole through at one time. How many Gillettes is you new toy?
